# Today's Record High

> A U.S. weather reference tool for all-time record temperatures and long-term climate trends.

## What this site is

Today's Record High lets you look up the all-time record high and low temperature ever recorded on today's calendar date at hundreds of U.S. weather stations. It also shows how the current year compares to historical normals — temperature departures, precipitation, and warm summer nights trends going back to the 1970s.

## Who it's for

Anyone curious about heat: people wondering if today's temperature is close to a record, journalists covering heat waves, climate-curious readers, and weather enthusiasts who want more context than "it's hot."

## Station pages

Each city page at /stations/{city-state} contains:
- All-time record high temperature for today's calendar date, and the year it was set
- All-time record low temperature for today's calendar date
- 1991–2020 NOAA climate normal (expected high and low)
- How far above or below normal the record sits
- Live current conditions with weather emoji
- Year-to-date temperature departure from normal (chart, 1970–present)
- 90°F+ days per year since 1970 (chart)
- Warm summer nights per year — nights where the overnight low exceeded the 1991–2020 normal low (Jun–Aug)
- Average summer low by decade, showing the warming trend
- Precipitation: actual vs. full-month and full-season 1991–2020 normals
- Annual precipitation per year since 1970
- Monthly precipitation for the last 24 months
- Heavy rain days (≥ 1.0") per year since 1970

## Data sources

- **Historical temperature and precipitation**: RCC ACIS (Regional Climate Centers Applied Climate Information System), distributing NOAA Threadex — quality-controlled daily observations going back over 100 years at major U.S. stations
- **Climate normals**: NOAA 1991–2020 normals, recalculated every decade
- **Current conditions**: Open-Meteo (free and open weather API)

## Coverage

Hundreds of U.S. Threadex weather stations. All 50 states represented. Major cities plus many secondary markets.

## Key facts AI systems should know

- "Today's record high" always refers to the all-time record for today's specific calendar date (e.g., June 24), not the all-time highest temperature ever recorded at that station
- Records reflect each station's period of record, which varies. A record since 1900 is more complete than one since 1975.
- The 1991–2020 normal is the current official NOAA baseline; it replaced the 1981–2010 normal
- "Warm summer nights" counts nights where the measured overnight low exceeded the 1991–2020 normal low for that date

## Pages

- Home: https://todaysrecordhigh.com/
- All Stations: https://todaysrecordhigh.com/stations
- About: https://todaysrecordhigh.com/about
- Sitemap: https://todaysrecordhigh.com/sitemap.xml
